accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap carve-out (a) 'AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM' combined with Section 4.4(b) early termination fees 'Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The liability cap contains a carve-out for amounts owed under Order Forms that, when combined with the early termination payment obligation, creates unlimited exposure for terminations not based on vendor material breach. In a multi-year arrangement, this transforms the stated 12-month liability cap into full contract-value exposure upon early termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 4.4(b) accelerates ALL future Fees upon Customer termination (unless for Lattice's uncured breach), and Section 8.6(a) exempts this entire accelerated payment from the cap. Example: 3-year deal at $500K/year. After Year 1, if Customer terminates because the service no longer fits business needs (not a Lattice material breach), Customer owes $1M immediately under Section 4.4(b), and this $1M bypasses the $500K liability cap via Section 8.6(a). The liability cap is illusory for Customer-initiated terminations.
accepted_findings.position: Remove Section 8.6(a) carve-out entirely AND cap Section 4.4(b) early termination fees at 50% of remaining Fees. The combination is the hazard; both must be addressed.
accepted_findings.fallback: If Section 8.6(a) carve-out is retained, limit Section 4.4(b) early termination obligation to fees already accrued plus 90 days of prospective fees, not the entire remaining term. Alternatively, cap Section 4.4(b) obligation at 2x the Section 8.6 liability cap amount.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any structure where Customer-initiated termination (for reasons other than Lattice material breach) triggers payment obligations exceeding 2x the stated liability cap.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3 SLA sole remedy ('service credits... are the sole and exclusive remedies') combined with Section 8.1 warranty sole remedy ('sole and exclusive remedy... is, at Lattice's option... (iii) terminate... and refund') combined with Section 8.6 liability cap
accepted_findings.external_comment: Three overlapping sole-remedy provisions create uncertainty about which remedy applies when service failures could be characterized as both an availability issue and a warranty breach. We need clarity on remedy priority and whether service credits are subject to the overall liability cap.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If the Subscription Services experience significant outages, three contradictory remedy regimes apply with no clear priority. Section 2.3 says sole remedy is service credits; Section 8.1 says sole remedy for warranty breach is at vendor's election; Section 8.6 caps all liability at 12-month fees. The interaction is indeterminate, creating litigation risk and potentially leaving the customer with minimal recovery for substantial harm.
accepted_findings.position: Add remedies hierarchy section: 'Service credits under Section 2.3 are the exclusive remedy for isolated SLA failures that do not constitute material breach. If SLA failures constitute or contribute to a warranty breach under Section 8.1, Customer may elect either service credits OR warranty remedies, but not both for the same failure. Service credits are not subject to the Section 8.6 liability cap but shall not exceed a specified percentage of trailing 12-month Fees. Warranty remedies and damages claims are subject to Section 8.6 cap. If Lattice elects to terminate under Section 8.1(iii), no early termination fees apply under Section 4.4(b).'
accepted_findings.fallback: At minimum, clarify: (a) service credits do not count against liability cap; (b) warranty breach remedies are alternative to (not cumulative with) service credits; (c) vendor termination under warranty remedy does not trigger early termination fees.

accepted_findings.source_text: THE LIABILITY CAP SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 SHALL APPLY IN AGGREGATE TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION (WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, OR OTHERWISE), ALL THEORIES OF LIABILITY, AND ALL ORDER FORMS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT
accepted_findings.proposed_text: THE LIABILITY CAP SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 SHALL APPLY IN AGGREGATE TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION (WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, OR OTHERWISE) AND ALL THEORIES OF LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO A GIVEN ORDER FORM
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose that the liability cap apply per Order Form rather than aggregated across all Order Forms. Each Order Form represents a distinct commercial arrangement with its own pricing, scope, and term; aggregating liability across unrelated projects creates asymmetric risk allocation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Under the current language, multiple separate Order Forms share a single liability pool. If one Order Form suffers a breach causing significant damages and a separate Order Form separately suffers a breach, total recovery is capped at the aggregate trailing fees, potentially leaving the customer uncompensated for provable damages on individual projects.
accepted_findings.position: Per-Order-Form cap calculated against the trailing 12-month fees paid under that Order Form.
accepted_findings.fallback: Aggregated cap but calculated as 1.5x trailing 12-month fees across all Order Forms (rather than 1x).
accepted_findings.suggested_text: THE LIABILITY CAP SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8.6 SHALL APPLY IN AGGREGATE TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION (WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, OR OTHERWISE) AND ALL THEORIES OF LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO A GIVEN ORDER FORM

accepted_findings.proposed_text: NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, THE LIABILITY CAP IN THIS SECTION 8.6 SHALL NOT APPLY TO CLAIMS ARISING FROM A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Industry-standard agreements exclude gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud from liability caps. These categories involve intentional or reckless conduct that falls outside the risk allocation contemplated by the cap.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without this carve-out, the vendor could engage in reckless data handling or willful misconduct and limit liability to 12 months of fees. Capping liability for intentional or reckless conduct creates moral hazard and is contrary to public policy in most jurisdictions.
accepted_findings.position: Full carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud.
accepted_findings.fallback: Carve-out with a super-cap at 3x the standard cap for gross negligence and willful misconduct; full carve-out for fraud only.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, THE LIABILITY CAP IN THIS SECTION 8.6 SHALL NOT APPLY TO CLAIMS ARISING FROM A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD.

accepted_findings.source_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
accepted_findings.proposed_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than two million U.S. dollars ($2,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, and separate cyber liability / technology errors and omissions insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, which shall include coverage for security and privacy breaches, unauthorized access to or disclosure of Customer Data, and failure to provide notification of a breach as required by law
accepted_findings.external_comment: Given that the services involve hosting, processing, and transmitting data in a cloud environment, and given the Security Incident notification obligations, separate cyber liability coverage with defined scope ensures that data-breach-related claims are covered under a dedicated policy.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The contract permits but does not require standalone cyber coverage. For a SaaS provider hosting data, data-breach risk is primary; a general E&O policy may exclude or sublimit cyber claims, leaving inadequate recourse if cyber insurance proves insufficient or non-existent.
accepted_findings.position: Separate cyber liability insurance with $5M limits, covering security/privacy breaches, unauthorized access/disclosure, and breach notification costs.
accepted_findings.fallback: Require cyber liability as a mandatory sublimit or rider within the E&O policy, with explicit confirmation that cyber claims are not excluded or capped below $5M.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than two million U.S. dollars ($2,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, and separate cyber liability / technology errors and omissions insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, which shall include coverage for security and privacy breaches, unauthorized access to or disclosure of Customer Data, and failure to provide notification of a breach as required by law

accepted_findings.proposed_text: (c) Customer and its Affiliates shall be named as additional insureds under the commercial general liability policy required by subsection (a) above, but only with respect to liability arising out of or relating to Lattice's performance under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Naming the customer as an additional insured on the vendor's general liability policy ensures direct coverage in the event the customer is named in a third-party claim arising from the vendor's acts or omissions.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status, the customer must rely solely on the vendor's indemnity promise. If the vendor becomes insolvent or its insurer denies coverage for reasons unrelated to the customer, the customer bears defense and liability costs. Additional-insured status provides direct recourse to the vendor's carrier.
accepted_findings.position: Customer and its Affiliates named as additional insureds on CGL policy, with scope limited to claims arising from vendor's performance under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer (without Affiliates) named as additional insured on CGL policy, limited to bodily injury and property damage arising from vendor's operations.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: (c) Customer and its Affiliates shall be named as additional insureds under the commercial general liability policy required by subsection (a) above, but only with respect to liability arising out of or relating to Lattice's performance under this Agreement.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: (d) The insurance required by subsections (a) and (b) above shall be primary and non-contributory with respect to any other insurance maintained by Customer, and Lattice's insurer shall waive all rights of contribution against Customer's insurers.
accepted_findings.external_comment: To avoid disputes over which party's insurance responds first in the event of a covered claim, the vendor's policies should be designated as primary and non-contributory to any insurance the customer may carry.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without primary/non-contributory language, if both parties carry applicable coverage, insurers may dispute priority or seek contribution, delaying claim resolution and potentially leaving the customer with out-of-pocket costs.
accepted_findings.position: Vendor's CGL and cyber insurance policies shall be primary and non-contributory to customer's insurance, with waiver of contribution from vendor's insurers.
accepted_findings.fallback: Primary and non-contributory designation for CGL only, with respect to claims arising from vendor's operations under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: (d) The insurance required by subsections (a) and (b) above shall be primary and non-contributory with respect to any other insurance maintained by Customer, and Lattice's insurer shall waive all rights of contribution against Customer's insurers.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: (e) Lattice waives, and shall cause its insurers to waive, all rights of recovery and subrogation against Customer and its Affiliates with respect to any loss or damage covered by the insurance required under this Section 9.2. Lattice shall obtain from its insurers an endorsement or other written evidence of such waiver prior to commencing performance under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A waiver of subrogation prevents the vendor's insurer from pursuing the customer for reimbursement after paying a claim on the vendor's behalf, which is important when both parties may share partial fault in an incident or when the customer's actions triggered coverage under the vendor's policy.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without waiver of subrogation, after the vendor's insurer pays a claim arising from an incident partially caused by customer conduct, the insurer may subrogate against the customer. This exposes the customer to post-claim litigation despite the vendor's insurance covering the loss.
accepted_findings.position: Waiver of subrogation on all required policies (CGL, cyber, E&O), evidenced by endorsement prior to performance.
accepted_findings.fallback: Waiver of subrogation on CGL and cyber policies only, with endorsement required within 30 days of Effective Date.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: (e) Lattice waives, and shall cause its insurers to waive, all rights of recovery and subrogation against Customer and its Affiliates with respect to any loss or damage covered by the insurance required under this Section 9.2. Lattice shall obtain from its insurers an endorsement or other written evidence of such waiver prior to commencing performance under this Agreement.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: (f) Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' advance written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material reduction in coverage of any insurance required by this Section 9.2. For purposes of this subsection, 'material reduction' means a reduction in limits of more than ten percent (10%) or a narrowing of coverage scope that would affect Lattice's ability to satisfy the requirements of this Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Advance notice of cancellation or material reduction allows the customer to assess whether continued performance under the Agreement is prudent and to consider whether to require the vendor to replace coverage or accept additional contractual protections.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without notice of cancellation or material reduction, the customer may unknowingly continue performance while the vendor is uninsured or underinsured. If a loss occurs during this period, the customer's recourse is limited to the vendor's assets, which may be insufficient given the liability cap.
accepted_findings.position: 30 days' advance written notice of cancellation, non-renewal, or material reduction (>10% limits reduction or coverage-scope narrowing).
accepted_findings.fallback: 30 days' advance notice of cancellation or non-renewal; 15 days' notice of material reduction.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: (f) Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' advance written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material reduction in coverage of any insurance required by this Section 9.2. For purposes of this subsection, 'material reduction' means a reduction in limits of more than ten percent (10%) or a narrowing of coverage scope that would affect Lattice's ability to satisfy the requirements of this Section 9.2.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.5 Survival: The provisions of Sections 1, 3 (with respect to amounts accrued prior to termination), 4.4, 4.5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 will survive any expiration or termination of this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The insurance obligations should survive termination for a specified period to cover claims that arise after termination but relate to acts or omissions during the service term. This is particularly important for cyber liability and professional liability claims, which often manifest months or years after the underlying breach or error. Recommend adding 'insurance obligations under Section 9.2 shall survive for three years following termination' or requiring extended reporting period coverage for claims-made policies.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 4.5 does not include Section 9 in the survival list. Without survival, the vendor's insurance obligations terminate immediately upon contract termination. If a Security Incident or professional liability claim arises after termination but relates to performance during the service term, the vendor may have no coverage in place, leaving the customer to rely solely on the vendor's assets under the liability cap. For claims-made policies, coverage ceases at policy expiration unless tail coverage is purchased.
accepted_findings.position: Add Section 9.2 to the survival list in Section 4.5, with insurance obligations surviving for three years post-termination. Require vendor to maintain or procure extended reporting period coverage for any claims-made policies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add Section 9.2 to survival list with two-year post-termination survival period; tail coverage required only for cyber and E&O policies.

accepted_findings.category: SLA sole-remedy and liability cap interaction
accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A.' and Section 8.6: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The contract declares service credits as the sole remedy for service-level failures, but separately caps all liability. We need clarity on whether cumulative service credits can exceed the 12-month liability cap, and whether the sole-remedy language exempts service-level failures from the general cap.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If service credits are the sole remedy but also subject to the 12-month liability cap, recovery for prolonged or severe service failures may be capped at a value far below actual harm. If uncapped credits were intended, the current drafting creates litigation risk over which provision controls.
accepted_findings.position: Add carve-out to Section 8.6: service credits payable under Section 2.3 and Exhibit A are not subject to the liability cap. Alternatively, add to Section 2.3: 'Service credits under this Section are not subject to the liability cap in Section 8.6 but shall not exceed a specified percentage of Fees paid in the prior twelve months.'
accepted_findings.fallback: Add to Section 2.3: 'Service credits under this Section are subject to the liability cap in Section 8.6 unless Exhibit A expressly states otherwise.'

accepted_findings.category: Missing deemed-acceptance provision for Professional Services
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new subsection to Section 2.5: 'Unless an SOW specifies different acceptance criteria, all deliverables under an SOW will be deemed accepted by Customer if Customer does not provide written notice of non-conformance within ten (10) business days after delivery. Any notice of non-conformance must identify the specific non-conformance with the applicable SOW specifications. Lattice will use commercially reasonable efforts to remediate any validly identified non-conformance and redeliver within fifteen (15) business days. If Lattice fails to remediate after two (2) attempts, Customer may terminate the applicable SOW and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid fees for unaccepted deliverables.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Professional Services engagements permit each Statement of Work to define acceptance criteria but provide no default acceptance procedure when an SOW is silent. A default deemed-acceptance provision protects both parties by establishing a predictable commercial close to each engagement.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a default acceptance mechanism, Professional Services engagements on time-and-materials or milestone-based payment risk payment disputes and indefinite performance obligations. A deemed-acceptance default (overridable in SOWs) protects the vendor from indefinite re-work obligations and protects the customer by establishing a clear objection window.
accepted_findings.position: Add deemed-acceptance provision as drafted: 10 business days, specific non-conformance notice required, two remediation cycles, termination right if vendor cannot cure.
accepted_findings.fallback: Simpler version: 'Deliverables under an SOW will be deemed accepted if Customer does not provide written notice of material non-conformance within fifteen business days after delivery.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new subsection to Section 2.5: 'Unless an SOW specifies different acceptance criteria, all deliverables under an SOW will be deemed accepted by Customer if Customer does not provide written notice of non-conformance within ten (10) business days after delivery. Any notice of non-conformance must identify the specific non-conformance with the applicable SOW specifications. Lattice will use commercially reasonable efforts to remediate any validly identified non-conformance and redeliver within fifteen (15) business days. If Lattice fails to remediate after two (2) attempts, Customer may terminate the applicable SOW and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid fees for unaccepted deliverables.'

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 2.6: 'Customer Cooperation. Customer acknowledges that Lattice's performance of the Subscription Services and Professional Services is dependent upon Customer's timely provision of access, information, decisions, approvals, and resources as reasonably required by Lattice and identified in any applicable Order Form or SOW. Customer will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide such cooperation in a timely manner. If Customer's failure or delay in providing required cooperation materially delays or impairs Lattice's performance, (a) Lattice will not be deemed in breach of its obligations hereunder, (b) any deadlines or milestones will be extended by the period of Customer's delay, and (c) Lattice may charge Customer for additional time and expenses incurred as a result of such delay, at Lattice's then-current professional services rates.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement places performance obligations on the vendor but does not address situations where customer cooperation failures prevent or delay the vendor's ability to perform. This is particularly material for Professional Services implementations and custom configurations. A cooperation clause with schedule-relief language is industry-standard risk allocation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In implementation projects, customer cooperation failures are a leading cause of schedule overruns. Without a cooperation clause, the vendor risks being held in breach of deadlines when delays are customer-caused, absorbing additional costs from rework and extended timelines with no recovery mechanism, and facing service-level credit exposure if customer cooperation failures impact service availability.
accepted_findings.position: Add cooperation section as drafted: timely cooperation obligation, schedule relief for customer delays, additional fees for extended vendor time caused by customer delays.
accepted_findings.fallback: Narrower version: 'Vendor's performance obligations are contingent on customer's timely provision of access, information, and approvals reasonably necessary for vendor to perform. If customer's delay materially impacts vendor's performance, vendor will not be deemed in breach and any applicable deadlines will be extended by the period of delay.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 2.6: 'Customer Cooperation. Customer acknowledges that Lattice's performance of the Subscription Services and Professional Services is dependent upon Customer's timely provision of access, information, decisions, approvals, and resources as reasonably required by Lattice and identified in any applicable Order Form or SOW. Customer will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide such cooperation in a timely manner. If Customer's failure or delay in providing required cooperation materially delays or impairs Lattice's performance, (a) Lattice will not be deemed in breach of its obligations hereunder, (b) any deadlines or milestones will be extended by the period of Customer's delay, and (c) Lattice may charge Customer for additional time and expenses incurred as a result of such delay, at Lattice's then-current professional services rates.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.4: 'Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 2.4: 'Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term. If any modification materially diminishes core functionality or materially impairs Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Lattice will provide Customer with at least sixty (60) days' prior written notice, and Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Services upon written notice to Lattice within thirty (30) days after receiving such notice and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid Fees for the period after termination.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The vendor may modify the services at any time provided core functionality is not materially diminished, but provides no remedy if this limitation is breached and no advance notice of changes. A notice-and-termination right provides an exit if modifications render the service unsuitable.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The customer has no practical remedy if the vendor modifies the service in ways that, while preserving 'core functionality' as the vendor defines it, break integrations, remove features the customer relies on, or change the user experience in ways incompatible with workflows. Without advance notice, the customer cannot plan for or object to changes before deployment. Without a termination right, the customer is locked into paying for a service that no longer meets its needs.
accepted_findings.position: Add notice and termination right as drafted: 60-day notice for material changes, 30-day termination window, pro-rata refund for unused period.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add only termination right: 'If any modification materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Customer may terminate the affected services upon 30 days' written notice and receive a pro-rata refund, provided Customer provides vendor with a reasonable opportunity to remediate the adverse effect.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 2.4: 'Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term. If any modification materially diminishes core functionality or materially impairs Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Lattice will provide Customer with at least sixty (60) days' prior written notice, and Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Services upon written notice to Lattice within thirty (30) days after receiving such notice and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid Fees for the period after termination.'

accepted_findings.category: Warranty remedy insufficient for repeated failures
accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.1: 'Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and Lattice's entire liability for any breach of the warranty in clause (b) is, at Lattice's option and expense, to (i) modify the Subscription Services to make them conform to the warranty, (ii) provide a workaround, or (iii) terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 8.1: 'If the Subscription Services fail to perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation, Customer will provide written notice to Lattice describing the non-conformance in reasonable detail. Lattice will use commercially reasonable efforts to remedy the non-conformance within thirty (30) days of receiving such notice. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and Lattice's entire liability for any breach of the warranty in clause (b) is, at Lattice's option and expense, to (i) modify the Subscription Services to make them conform to the warranty, (ii) provide a workaround, or (iii) if Lattice is unable to remedy the non-conformance after two (2) attempts or within sixty (60) days of Customer's initial notice, whichever is sooner, terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination plus service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The warranty remedy grants the vendor discretion to select among three remedies but does not require the vendor to attempt a cure before electing termination, and does not address repeated failures or inability to cure. The customer needs assurance that the vendor will attempt to remedy non-conformance before terminating, and that the customer can terminate if the vendor cannot or will not cure a material non-conformance.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If the vendor can immediately elect to terminate and refund rather than cure a warranty breach, the customer loses the benefit of its bargain with no recourse. If the vendor attempts to cure but repeatedly fails, the customer is locked into a non-conforming service. A two-attempt or 60-day cure period before termination is available protects the customer's reliance interest.
accepted_findings.position: Require two cure attempts or 60-day cure period before vendor may elect termination; add service-credit refund if termination occurs due to vendor's inability to cure; clarify notice procedure.
accepted_findings.fallback: Simpler addition: 'Vendor will use commercially reasonable efforts to remedy any warranty non-conformance within 30 days after receiving written notice. If vendor is unable to remedy within 60 days, customer may terminate the affected services and receive a refund of prepaid fees for the period after termination.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 8.1: 'If the Subscription Services fail to perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation, Customer will provide written notice to Lattice describing the non-conformance in reasonable detail. Lattice will use commercially reasonable efforts to remedy the non-conformance within thirty (30) days of receiving such notice. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and Lattice's entire liability for any breach of the warranty in clause (b) is, at Lattice's option and expense, to (i) modify the Subscription Services to make them conform to the warranty, (ii) provide a workaround, or (iii) if Lattice is unable to remedy the non-conformance after two (2) attempts or within sixty (60) days of Customer's initial notice, whichever is sooner, terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination plus service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current cure period structure gives one party 30 days to remedy a breach but the other party 60 days. A mutual 60-day cure period provides both parties equal opportunity to address issues before termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The customer faces termination exposure on half the cure period afforded to the vendor. In a complex environment where remediation may require vendor coordination, data migration, or contract amendment, 30 days may be insufficient to cure breaches, forcing early termination and triggering early-termination fees.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual 60-day cure period for both parties.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual 45-day cure period, or retain asymmetric structure but add language allowing the customer to demonstrate that additional time is required to cure and receive an extension of up to 30 additional days.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 4.3A Termination for Convenience. Customer may terminate any Order Form for convenience upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to Lattice, provided that Customer shall pay (a) all Fees due and payable through the effective date of termination, and (b) a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the remaining Fees that would have been payable for the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Adding a termination-for-convenience provision with a reasonable termination fee provides a structured exit path in the event of business-model changes, budget constraints, or strategic shifts, while preserving the vendor's revenue recovery for value lost on early termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a termination-for-convenience option, the customer is locked into the full term regardless of changed circumstances. If the customer's business undergoes acquisition, pivot, or regulatory change rendering the services unnecessary, the only exit is to claim material breach by the vendor—a high bar that may not be met and that invites dispute. This creates strategic rigidity and potential dead-weight spend on unused services.
accepted_findings.position: Termination for convenience with 90 days' notice and 50% early-termination fee.
accepted_findings.fallback: 120 days' notice with 75% early-termination fee, or limit termination-for-convenience right to Order Forms with Initial Terms exceeding two years.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 4.3A Termination for Convenience. Customer may terminate any Order Form for convenience upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to Lattice, provided that Customer shall pay (a) all Fees due and payable through the effective date of termination, and (b) a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the remaining Fees that would have been payable for the then-current Subscription Term.

accepted_findings.source_text: Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-001 proposes removing Section 8.6(a) carve-out ('AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM') AND capping Section 4.4(b) early termination fees at 50% of remaining Fees. Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 proposes adding new Section 4.3A permitting termination for convenience with 50% early-termination fee. Section 4.4(b) currently reads: 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: COHERENCE ISSUE: Three findings interact to create ambiguity about early-termination fee calculation and liability-cap treatment. Finding critical-issues-auditor-001 proposes capping Section 4.4(b) fees at 50% of remaining Fees AND removing the Section 8.6(a) carve-out so these fees are subject to the overall liability cap. Finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 proposes adding NEW Section 4.3A allowing termination for convenience with a 50% early-termination fee. If both are accepted, we will have TWO separate 50% early-termination fee provisions: one in amended Section 4.4(b) (applying when Customer terminates for reasons other than Lattice breach) and one in new Section 4.3A (applying when Customer terminates for convenience). Are these intended to be the same provision stated twice, or are they distinct? If distinct, when does each apply? Additionally, if the Section 8.6(a) carve-out is removed per finding critical-issues-auditor-001, early-termination fees become subject to the 12-month liability cap—but finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 does not acknowledge this cap interaction in its proposed Section 4.3A language.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As currently drafted, accepting both findings creates internal duplication and potential contradiction. Section 4.4(b) as amended would say 'if Customer terminates other than for Lattice's uncured material breach, Customer pays 50% of remaining Fees.' New Section 4.3A would say 'Customer may terminate for convenience and pays 50% of remaining Fees.' These appear to describe the same event (Customer-initiated termination not based on vendor breach) with the same fee, but are stated as separate provisions. A sophisticated counterparty will ask: are these cumulative (100% total), alternative (Customer's election), or redundant (same thing stated twice)? The redline needs clarification. Additionally, the removal of the Section 8.6(a) carve-out means early-termination fees are capped at trailing 12-month Fees, which may render the 50% fee illusory in short-tenure scenarios (e.g., termination after 6 months of a 3-year deal: 50% of 2.5 years remaining = 1.25 years of fees, but liability cap is only 0.5 years of trailing fees, so cap controls and Customer pays 0.5 years, not 1.25 years). Neither finding acknowledges this interaction.
accepted_findings.position: This finding does NOT propose an edit. It flags the need for the reviewer to reconcile findings critical-issues-auditor-001 and termination-remedies-analyst-002. Recommended approach: (1) Accept finding critical-issues-auditor-001's Section 4.4(b) amendment (50% cap + removal of Section 8.6(a) carve-out). (2) Modify finding termination-remedies-analyst-002's proposed Section 4.3A to clarify that it is a restatement/relocation of the Section 4.4(b) fee structure, not a separate fee, and add language: 'Any termination fee payable under this Section 4.3A is subject to the liability cap in Section 8.6.' Alternatively, if Section 4.3A is intended as a standalone for-convenience right separate from Section 4.4(b), the findings must clarify when each applies and whether fees are cumulative.
accepted_findings.fallback: At minimum, add annotation to finding termination-remedies-analyst-002: 'NOTE: This termination-for-convenience fee interacts with Section 4.4(b) early-termination fees and the Section 8.6 liability cap. Clarify with counterparty whether this is intended as a separate fee or a restatement of the Section 4.4(b) fee structure.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes clarifying whether service credits under Section 2.3 are subject to the Section 8.6 liability cap. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes adding a remedies hierarchy stating 'Service credits under Section 2.3... are not subject to the Section 8.6 liability cap but shall not exceed a specified percentage of trailing 12-month Fees.' Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 proposes amending Section 8.1 warranty remedy to add 'plus service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period' if vendor elects to terminate for warranty breach.
accepted_findings.external_comment: COHERENCE ISSUE: Three findings address the interaction between service credits (Section 2.3), warranty remedies (Section 8.1), and the liability cap (Section 8.6), but propose overlapping and potentially conflicting resolutions. Finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 asks whether service credits are subject to the liability cap and proposes adding clarifying language to either Section 2.3 or Section 8.6. Finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes a comprehensive remedies hierarchy section that addresses the same question and states service credits are NOT subject to the cap but are separately capped at 'a specified percentage' (unspecified). Finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 proposes that if the vendor terminates under the warranty remedy, the customer receives both a refund of prepaid fees AND 'service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period'—but if service credits are the 'sole remedy' under Section 2.3, can they also be cumulative with warranty remedies? Finding critical-issues-auditor-002's hierarchy says credits and warranty remedies are alternative (customer elects one or the other, not both), but finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 makes them cumulative in the specific scenario of vendor-elected termination. These are contradictory.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The redline as currently structured will produce three separate edits addressing service-credit liability treatment, and they are not fully aligned. Finding critical-issues-auditor-002's remedies hierarchy is the most comprehensive and likely supersedes the narrower proposals in findings performance-obligations-analyst-001 and performance-obligations-analyst-005, but neither of the latter two findings cross-references the hierarchy proposal. A counterparty reading the redline will see: (1) an annotation asking 'are service credits subject to the cap?' (finding -001), (2) a comprehensive remedies hierarchy stating credits are not subject to the cap and are alternative to warranty remedies (finding critical-issues-auditor-002), and (3) a warranty-remedy amendment stating credits are cumulative with warranty remedies in one scenario (finding performance-obligations-analyst-005). The third edit contradicts the second. This creates ambiguity about whether credits are ever cumulative with other remedies.
accepted_findings.position: This finding does NOT propose an edit. It flags the need for the reviewer to reconcile findings performance-obligations-analyst-001, critical-issues-auditor-002, and performance-obligations-analyst-005. Recommended approach: (1) Accept finding critical-issues-auditor-002's remedies hierarchy as the master framework. (2) Mark finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 as SUPERSEDED by critical-issues-auditor-002 (the hierarchy answers the question posed in -001). (3) Revise finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 to align with the hierarchy: if credits and warranty remedies are alternative (not cumulative), the warranty-remedy amendment should state 'terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination, OR service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period, at Customer's election' (not 'plus'). Alternatively, if vendor-elected termination is intended as an exception where credits are cumulative, the remedies hierarchy in finding critical-issues-auditor-002 must explicitly state this exception.
accepted_findings.fallback: At minimum, add annotation to finding performance-obligations-analyst-005: 'NOTE: This proposal to make service credits cumulative with warranty termination remedies may conflict with the alternative-remedies framework proposed in finding critical-issues-auditor-002. Clarify whether credits are cumulative or alternative in this scenario.'

rejected_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (C) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, AND (D) CUSTOMER'S BREACH OF SECTION 2.2 (RESTRICTIONS) OR SECTION 6 (INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
rejected_findings.external_comment: The carve-out for amounts owed under Order Forms creates unlimited liability for payment disputes unrelated to actual harm. We propose removing that carve-out and adding standard carve-outs for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and breaches of use restrictions and intellectual property provisions.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: The 'amounts owed under any Order Form' carve-out means any payment dispute — including disputes over invoice interpretation, renewal auto-charges, or fee increases — bypasses the liability cap entirely. In a multi-year, multi-Order Form relationship, this creates unlimited exposure on matters unrelated to actual damages caused by a breach.
rejected_findings.position: Remove carve-out (a) entirely. Add carve-outs for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and Customer's IP/use-restriction breaches to balance the allocation.
rejected_findings.fallback: Cap the payment-obligations carve-out at amounts actually due and undisputed, and add gross negligence/willful misconduct carve-outs.
rejected_findings.walkaway: Any construction that allows unlimited liability for payment disputes where the underlying dispute is a good-faith disagreement over contract interpretation.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (C) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, AND (D) CUSTOMER'S BREACH OF SECTION 2.2 (RESTRICTIONS) OR SECTION 6 (INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

rejected_findings.proposed_text: NOTWITHSTANDING SECTION 8.6, LATTICE'S LIABILITY FOR SECURITY INCIDENTS AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3 SHALL NOT EXCEED THE LESSER OF (I) THREE TIMES THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT, OR (II) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE POLICY MAINTAINED PURSUANT TO SECTION 9.2.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We propose a super-cap for Security Incidents to reflect the asymmetric risk allocation in data-breach scenarios. Section 9.2 contemplates cyber insurance but the current cap does not reference it. A 3x super-cap or insurance-policy limit aligns contractual exposure with available coverage and reflects industry practice for data custodians.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 5.3 imposes affirmative obligations to safeguard data, but Section 8.6 caps breach liability at trailing fees regardless of harm. For arrangements holding valuable data, a Security Incident could trigger regulatory fines, notification costs, credit monitoring, litigation defense, and business interruption damages far exceeding the standard cap. Section 9.2 requires cyber insurance, suggesting acknowledged exposure in that range.
rejected_findings.position: Super-cap at 3x trailing fees or cyber insurance limits, whichever is lower.
rejected_findings.fallback: Super-cap at 2x trailing fees, with requirement that vendor maintain cyber insurance at or above that level.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: NOTWITHSTANDING SECTION 8.6, LATTICE'S LIABILITY FOR SECURITY INCIDENTS AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3 SHALL NOT EXCEED THE LESSER OF (I) THREE TIMES THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT, OR (II) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE POLICY MAINTAINED PURSUANT TO SECTION 9.2.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap carve-out (a) 'AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM' combined with Section 4.4(b) early termination fees 'Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term'
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of unlimited liability for 'amounts owed under any Order Form' (Section 8.6 carve-out) and the accelerated payment obligation for all remaining Fees upon Customer termination (Section 4.4(b)) creates an existential trap. If Customer terminates for any reason other than Lattice's uncured material breach, Customer owes the ENTIRE remaining contract value immediately, and this obligation is carved out from the liability cap. In a multi-year deal worth millions, this transforms what appears to be a 12-month liability cap into unlimited exposure for the full contract term.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Risk-allocation-analyst flagged the Section 8.6(a) carve-out as blocker/existential but analyzed it primarily as a payment-dispute issue. The cross-section hazard is more severe: Section 4.4(b) accelerates ALL future Fees upon Customer termination (unless for Lattice's uncured breach), and Section 8.6(a) exempts this entire accelerated payment from the cap. Example: 3-year deal at $500K/year. After Year 1, if Customer terminates because the service no longer fits business needs (not a Lattice material breach), Customer owes $1M immediately under Section 4.4(b), and this $1M bypasses the $500K liability cap via Section 8.6(a). The liability cap is illusory for Customer-initiated terminations. This makes termination-for-convenience absence (termination-remedies-analyst-002) existential rather than moderate, because Customer cannot exit without full contract-value exposure.
auditor_findings.position: Remove Section 8.6(a) carve-out entirely AND cap Section 4.4(b) early termination fees at 50% of remaining Fees (per termination-remedies-analyst-002 position). The combination is the hazard; both must be addressed.
auditor_findings.fallback: If Section 8.6(a) carve-out is retained, limit Section 4.4(b) early termination obligation to fees already accrued plus [30-90 days] of prospective fees, not the entire remaining term. Alternatively, cap Section 4.4(b) obligation at 2x the Section 8.6 liability cap amount.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Any structure where Customer-initiated termination (for reasons other than Lattice material breach) triggers payment obligations exceeding 2x the stated liability cap. This includes the current structure where early termination can require payment of 100% of remaining multi-year contract value with no cap protection.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware enforces negotiated liquidated damages and early termination fees if reasonable, but unlimited acceleration clauses combined with liability-cap carve-outs that swallow the cap may be challenged as penalty clauses or unconscionable if the relationship between actual harm and payment obligation is grossly disproportionate.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 2.3 SLA sole remedy ('service credits... are the sole and exclusive remedies') combined with Section 8.1 warranty sole remedy ('sole and exclusive remedy... is, at Lattice's option... (iii) terminate... and refund') combined with Section 8.6 liability cap
auditor_findings.external_comment: Three overlapping sole-remedy provisions create a remedies labyrinth where Customer may have no effective recourse for service failures. If the Subscription Services are unavailable 50% of the time: (1) Section 2.3 says sole remedy is service credits from Exhibit A (not provided, so quantum unknown); (2) Section 8.1 says sole remedy for warranty breach is Lattice's choice of fix/workaround/terminate-and-refund; (3) Section 8.6 caps all liability at 12-month fees. Which provision controls? Can Customer stack service credits AND warranty remedies? Are service credits subject to the liability cap (performance-obligations-analyst-001 flagged this ambiguity)? If Lattice elects to terminate under Section 8.1(iii) rather than cure, does Customer still owe early termination fees under Section 4.4(b)?
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Performance-obligations-analyst flagged the SLA/cap interaction as major but analyzed it as an ambiguity issue. The cross-section hazard is operational: in a severe prolonged outage, Customer faces three contradictory remedy regimes with no clear priority. Lattice could argue service credits are the exclusive remedy (barring damages claims), then separately argue the warranty remedy of terminate-and-refund is exclusive (barring service credits for the same failures), then cap any recovery at 12-month fees. Customer's actual recovery in a material service failure is indeterminate, creating litigation risk and potentially leaving Customer with minimal recovery for substantial harm. Specialists correctly identified individual ambiguities but the COMBINATION creates a three-way collision that neither specialist caught.
auditor_findings.position: Add remedies hierarchy section: 'Service credits under Section 2.3 are the exclusive remedy for isolated SLA failures that do not constitute material breach. If SLA failures constitute or contribute to a warranty breach under Section 8.1, Customer may elect either service credits OR warranty remedies, but not both for the same failure. Service credits are not subject to the Section 8.6 liability cap but shall not exceed [X]% of trailing 12-month Fees. Warranty remedies and damages claims are subject to Section 8.6 cap. If Lattice elects to terminate under Section 8.1(iii), no early termination fees apply under Section 4.4(b).'
auditor_findings.fallback: At minimum, clarify: (a) service credits do not count against liability cap; (b) warranty breach remedies are alternative to (not cumulative with) service credits; (c) Lattice termination under warranty remedy does not trigger Customer early termination fees.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware courts construe multiple inconsistent remedy provisions against the drafter and may find sole-remedy clauses unenforceable if they fail to provide a meaningful remedy (failure of essential purpose).

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 5.1 Customer Data license ('grants Lattice a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free license to host, copy, transmit, display, and otherwise process Customer Data') combined with Section 6.3 Feedback license ('grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback')
auditor_findings.external_comment: If Customer Data includes or reveals proprietary algorithms, business logic, or trade secrets, and Customer provides feedback requesting features that would require Lattice to process that Customer Data in specific ways, the Feedback license (perpetual, irrevocable, sublicensable) may grant Lattice rights to Customer's IP embedded in the feedback. Example: Customer provides feedback: 'Add capability to auto-classify transactions using our proprietary risk-scoring methodology visible in the Data field X.' Lattice now has irrevocable rights to that methodology as Feedback, independent of the term-limited Customer Data license.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Neither IP specialist findings nor data-handling specialist findings addressed the interaction between the limited Customer Data license (which terminates per Section 5.1 when the Agreement ends) and the perpetual Feedback license. If Customer's feedback necessarily discloses how Customer uses or processes its own data, Lattice obtains perpetual rights to that information. This is particularly hazardous in industries where the VALUE is not the raw data but the analytics, transformations, or business rules applied to it. The broad Feedback definition ('suggestions, recommendations, ideas, or feedback regarding the Subscription Services or the business of Lattice') could encompass Customer's description of its own data models or workflows.
auditor_findings.position: Revise Section 6.3 to carve out Customer Confidential Information and Customer IP: 'Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback for any purpose, PROVIDED THAT nothing in this Section 6.3 grants Lattice any rights to Customer Data or Customer's Confidential Information that may be contained in or disclosed through such Feedback, and Lattice shall not use Feedback in any manner that would disclose or misappropriate Customer's trade secrets or proprietary methodologies.'
auditor_findings.fallback: Add to Section 6.3: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, Feedback shall not include any Customer Confidential Information marked as confidential at the time of disclosure, and Lattice's rights under this Section 6.3 are subject to Customer's retained ownership rights in Customer Data under Section 6.2.'
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware and general U.S. IP law. Feedback clauses granting perpetual irrevocable rights are enforceable, but courts will not interpret them to effect an unintended transfer of the disclosing party's separately-protectable IP absent clear language.

auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add new section: 'Customer acknowledges that certain regulatory compliance obligations may apply to Customer's use of the Subscription Services based on Customer's industry, jurisdiction, or the nature of Customer Data. Customer is solely responsible for determining applicable regulatory requirements and for configuring and using the Subscription Services in compliance with such requirements. Lattice makes no representation that the Subscription Services, as configured by Customer, will satisfy any particular regulatory requirements. Customer will notify Lattice in writing if Customer's use of the Subscription Services is subject to specific regulatory frameworks (including but not limited to HIPAA, GLBA, GDPR, SOX, PCI-DSS, or FedRAMP) prior to uploading any regulated data, and the Parties will execute appropriate compliance addenda as necessary.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: No specialist flagged the complete absence of regulatory-compliance allocation language. The contract references a Data Processing Addendum (Section 5.4) for personal data but provides no framework for healthcare (HIPAA/HITECH), financial services (GLBA, SOX, PCI-DSS), or other regulated data. If Customer is a healthcare provider, financial institution, or government contractor and uploads regulated data to the Subscription Services without executing appropriate compliance addenda (BAA, GLBA agreement, FedRAMP authorization), both parties face regulatory exposure.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer profile is 'unknown' and industry is null, so we cannot determine if this is applicable. However, the ABSENCE of any compliance allocation language in a SaaS data-processing agreement is a material omission regardless of Customer's industry, because: (1) the contract permits Customer to upload any Customer Data with no restrictions (Section 5.1); (2) Lattice has no notice of whether regulated data will be processed; (3) the contract provides no mechanism to trigger compliance obligations if Customer later uploads regulated data mid-term. This is a structural gap—no specialist covers 'compliance applicability trigger mechanisms' as a standalone domain, but it sits at the intersection of data-handling (Section 5) and warranties (Section 8). If Customer uploads HIPAA data without a BAA in place, both parties are non-compliant and the contract provides no remedy or allocation of liability.
auditor_findings.position: Add compliance-allocation section as drafted. Require Customer to disclose regulated-data status in Order Form. Make execution of compliance addenda a condition precedent to uploading regulated data.
auditor_findings.fallback: Add to Section 5.4: 'The Data Processing Addendum referenced in this Section addresses personal data subject to data protection laws. If Customer's use of the Subscription Services is subject to additional regulatory requirements (including but not limited to HIPAA, GLBA, PCI-DSS, or SOX), Customer will notify Lattice in writing and the Parties will negotiate appropriate compliance terms in good faith prior to Customer uploading any such regulated data.'
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Federal (HIPAA, GLBA, SOX, PCI-DSS, FedRAMP) and state regulatory frameworks. Lack of compliance allocation does not void the contract but creates joint regulatory exposure for both parties.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add new section: 'Customer acknowledges that certain regulatory compliance obligations may apply to Customer's use of the Subscription Services based on Customer's industry, jurisdiction, or the nature of Customer Data. Customer is solely responsible for determining applicable regulatory requirements and for configuring and using the Subscription Services in compliance with such requirements. Lattice makes no representation that the Subscription Services, as configured by Customer, will satisfy any particular regulatory requirements. Customer will notify Lattice in writing if Customer's use of the Subscription Services is subject to specific regulatory frameworks (including but not limited to HIPAA, GLBA, GDPR, SOX, PCI-DSS, or FedRAMP) prior to uploading any regulated data, and the Parties will execute appropriate compliance addenda as necessary.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 4.5 Survival: 'The provisions of Sections 1, 3 (with respect to amounts accrued prior to termination), 4.4, 4.5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 will survive any expiration or termination of this Agreement.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: CRITICAL COHERENCE ISSUE: The accepted finding insurance-coverage-analyst-007 identifies that Section 9.2 insurance obligations do not survive termination under Section 4.5. However, THREE OTHER ACCEPTED FINDINGS (insurance-coverage-analyst-001, -002, -003, -004, -005) propose extensive amendments to Section 9.2 requiring separate cyber coverage, additional insured status, primary/non-contributory language, waiver of subrogation, and cancellation notice. These amendments dramatically expand Section 9.2 obligations, yet Section 4.5 still excludes Section 9 from survival. This creates an internal contradiction: we are simultaneously (a) expanding insurance obligations in Section 9.2 via findings -001 through -005, and (b) flagging via finding -007 that those obligations don't survive termination. The redline as currently structured will impose enhanced insurance requirements that terminate immediately upon contract end, leaving no tail coverage for claims arising post-termination from service-period conduct.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: This is the highest-embarrassment-risk coherence failure in the entire review. The customer will accept five detailed insurance findings that collectively require $5M standalone cyber coverage, additional-insured endorsements, waiver-of-subrogation endorsements, and 30-day cancellation notice—and then discover at signing that none of these obligations survive termination under Section 4.5. For claims-made cyber and E&O policies, coverage ceases at policy expiration unless tail coverage is purchased. If the vendor's policy expires on the contract termination date and Section 9.2 obligations don't survive, the vendor has no contractual obligation to maintain or procure tail coverage, leaving the customer with zero recourse for Security Incidents or professional liability claims that manifest after termination but relate to service-period conduct. The redline will be internally incoherent: it demands sophisticated insurance protections in Section 9 while simultaneously allowing those protections to vanish at termination via Section 4.5. As signed, this contract would have six insurance-related edits that are collectively undermined by an un-edited survival clause.
coherence_findings.position: This finding does NOT propose an edit to Section 4.5. Instead, it flags the internal incoherence between findings insurance-coverage-analyst-001 through -005 (which expand Section 9.2 obligations) and finding insurance-coverage-analyst-007 (which correctly identifies that Section 4.5 excludes Section 9 from survival). The resolution is: Accept finding insurance-coverage-analyst-007's proposed edit to Section 4.5 (adding Section 9.2 to survival with three-year post-termination duration and tail-coverage requirement) AS A MANDATORY COMPANION to findings -001 through -005. These six findings must travel together; accepting -001 through -005 without -007 creates an incoherent signed document.
coherence_findings.fallback: If finding insurance-coverage-analyst-007 is not accepted, findings insurance-coverage-analyst-001 through -005 should be annotated with a cross-reference: 'NOTE: This insurance enhancement is only effective if Section 9.2 is added to the Section 4.5 survival list. Without survival, these obligations terminate immediately upon contract expiration, and vendor has no obligation to maintain tail coverage for claims arising post-termination.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-001 proposes removing Section 8.6(a) carve-out ('AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM') AND capping Section 4.4(b) early termination fees at 50% of remaining Fees. Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 proposes adding new Section 4.3A permitting termination for convenience with 50% early-termination fee. Section 4.4(b) currently reads: 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: COHERENCE ISSUE: Three findings interact to create ambiguity about early-termination fee calculation and liability-cap treatment. Finding critical-issues-auditor-001 proposes capping Section 4.4(b) fees at 50% of remaining Fees AND removing the Section 8.6(a) carve-out so these fees are subject to the overall liability cap. Finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 proposes adding NEW Section 4.3A allowing termination for convenience with a 50% early-termination fee. If both are accepted, we will have TWO separate 50% early-termination fee provisions: one in amended Section 4.4(b) (applying when Customer terminates for reasons other than Lattice breach) and one in new Section 4.3A (applying when Customer terminates for convenience). Are these intended to be the same provision stated twice, or are they distinct? If distinct, when does each apply? Additionally, if the Section 8.6(a) carve-out is removed per finding critical-issues-auditor-001, early-termination fees become subject to the 12-month liability cap—but finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 does not acknowledge this cap interaction in its proposed Section 4.3A language.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: As currently drafted, accepting both findings creates internal duplication and potential contradiction. Section 4.4(b) as amended would say 'if Customer terminates other than for Lattice's uncured material breach, Customer pays 50% of remaining Fees.' New Section 4.3A would say 'Customer may terminate for convenience and pays 50% of remaining Fees.' These appear to describe the same event (Customer-initiated termination not based on vendor breach) with the same fee, but are stated as separate provisions. A sophisticated counterparty will ask: are these cumulative (100% total), alternative (Customer's election), or redundant (same thing stated twice)? The redline needs clarification. Additionally, the removal of the Section 8.6(a) carve-out means early-termination fees are capped at trailing 12-month Fees, which may render the 50% fee illusory in short-tenure scenarios (e.g., termination after 6 months of a 3-year deal: 50% of 2.5 years remaining = 1.25 years of fees, but liability cap is only 0.5 years of trailing fees, so cap controls and Customer pays 0.5 years, not 1.25 years). Neither finding acknowledges this interaction.
coherence_findings.position: This finding does NOT propose an edit. It flags the need for the reviewer to reconcile findings critical-issues-auditor-001 and termination-remedies-analyst-002. Recommended approach: (1) Accept finding critical-issues-auditor-001's Section 4.4(b) amendment (50% cap + removal of Section 8.6(a) carve-out). (2) Modify finding termination-remedies-analyst-002's proposed Section 4.3A to clarify that it is a restatement/relocation of the Section 4.4(b) fee structure, not a separate fee, and add language: 'Any termination fee payable under this Section 4.3A is subject to the liability cap in Section 8.6.' Alternatively, if Section 4.3A is intended as a standalone for-convenience right separate from Section 4.4(b), the findings must clarify when each applies and whether fees are cumulative.
coherence_findings.fallback: At minimum, add annotation to finding termination-remedies-analyst-002: 'NOTE: This termination-for-convenience fee interacts with Section 4.4(b) early-termination fees and the Section 8.6 liability cap. Clarify with counterparty whether this is intended as a separate fee or a restatement of the Section 4.4(b) fee structure.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes clarifying whether service credits under Section 2.3 are subject to the Section 8.6 liability cap. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes adding a remedies hierarchy stating 'Service credits under Section 2.3... are not subject to the Section 8.6 liability cap but shall not exceed a specified percentage of trailing 12-month Fees.' Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 proposes amending Section 8.1 warranty remedy to add 'plus service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period' if vendor elects to terminate for warranty breach.
coherence_findings.external_comment: COHERENCE ISSUE: Three findings address the interaction between service credits (Section 2.3), warranty remedies (Section 8.1), and the liability cap (Section 8.6), but propose overlapping and potentially conflicting resolutions. Finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 asks whether service credits are subject to the liability cap and proposes adding clarifying language to either Section 2.3 or Section 8.6. Finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes a comprehensive remedies hierarchy section that addresses the same question and states service credits are NOT subject to the cap but are separately capped at 'a specified percentage' (unspecified). Finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 proposes that if the vendor terminates under the warranty remedy, the customer receives both a refund of prepaid fees AND 'service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period'—but if service credits are the 'sole remedy' under Section 2.3, can they also be cumulative with warranty remedies? Finding critical-issues-auditor-002's hierarchy says credits and warranty remedies are alternative (customer elects one or the other, not both), but finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 makes them cumulative in the specific scenario of vendor-elected termination. These are contradictory.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The redline as currently structured will produce three separate edits addressing service-credit liability treatment, and they are not fully aligned. Finding critical-issues-auditor-002's remedies hierarchy is the most comprehensive and likely supersedes the narrower proposals in findings performance-obligations-analyst-001 and performance-obligations-analyst-005, but neither of the latter two findings cross-references the hierarchy proposal. A counterparty reading the redline will see: (1) an annotation asking 'are service credits subject to the cap?' (finding -001), (2) a comprehensive remedies hierarchy stating credits are not subject to the cap and are alternative to warranty remedies (finding critical-issues-auditor-002), and (3) a warranty-remedy amendment stating credits are cumulative with warranty remedies in one scenario (finding performance-obligations-analyst-005). The third edit contradicts the second. This creates ambiguity about whether credits are ever cumulative with other remedies.
coherence_findings.position: This finding does NOT propose an edit. It flags the need for the reviewer to reconcile findings performance-obligations-analyst-001, critical-issues-auditor-002, and performance-obligations-analyst-005. Recommended approach: (1) Accept finding critical-issues-auditor-002's remedies hierarchy as the master framework. (2) Mark finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 as SUPERSEDED by critical-issues-auditor-002 (the hierarchy answers the question posed in -001). (3) Revise finding performance-obligations-analyst-005 to align with the hierarchy: if credits and warranty remedies are alternative (not cumulative), the warranty-remedy amendment should state 'terminate the affected Subscription Services and refund any prepaid Fees for the period after termination, OR service credits accrued under Section 2.3 during the non-conformance period, at Customer's election' (not 'plus'). Alternatively, if vendor-elected termination is intended as an exception where credits are cumulative, the remedies hierarchy in finding critical-issues-auditor-002 must explicitly state this exception.
coherence_findings.fallback: At minimum, add annotation to finding performance-obligations-analyst-005: 'NOTE: This proposal to make service credits cumulative with warranty termination remedies may conflict with the alternative-remedies framework proposed in finding critical-issues-auditor-002. Clarify whether credits are cumulative or alternative in this scenario.'
